name: Mock Test - 01
instruction:
1. The question paper contains three parts A, B and C.
  2. Section A consists of 20 questions of 1 mark each. Attempt any 16 questions.
  3. Section B consists of 20 questions of 1 mark each. Attempt any 16 questions.
  4. Section C consists of 10 questions based on two Case Studies. Attempt any 8 questions.
  5. There is no negative marking.

questions.question: The equation of a line parallel to x-axis and 5 units below the origin is
questions.explanation:
The equation of a line parallel to x-axis and 5 units below the origin is y = -5 because when a line parallel to x axis in that case equation of line is y = a.
Where a is the co-ordinate of y-axes and 5 units below the origin value x -coordinate is -5.
So required equation is y = -5

questions.question: In a given figure, if AB || CD || EF, PQ || RS, {tex}\angle{/tex}RQD = 25° and {tex}\angle{/tex}CQP = 60°, then {tex}\angle{/tex}QRS is equal to
questions.explanation:
Given, PQ || RS
{tex}\angle{/tex}PQC = {tex}\angle{/tex}BRS = 60° [alternate exterior angles and {tex}\angle{/tex}PQC = 60° (given)] and {tex}\angle{/tex}DQR = {tex}\angle{/tex}QRA = 25° [alternate interior angles]
[{tex}\angle{/tex}DQR = 25°, given]
{tex}\angle{/tex}QRS = {tex}\angle{/tex}QRA + {tex}\angle{/tex}ARS
= {tex}\angle{/tex}QRA + (180° – {tex}\angle{/tex}BRS) [linear pair axiom]
= 25° + 180° – 60°= 205° – 60°= 145°

questions.question: The height of an equilateral triangle is 6 cm, Then the area of the triangle is
questions.explanation:
Let each side of an equilateral triangle be 'a' units.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} its height {tex}=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \times \mathrm{a}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore 6=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \times \mathrm{a}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore a=\frac{12}{\sqrt{3}}{/tex}
{tex}=\left(\frac{12}{\sqrt{3}} \times \frac{\sqrt{3}}{\sqrt{3}}\right){/tex} ..... (Rationalizing the denominator)
{tex}=\left(\frac{12 \sqrt{3}}{3}\right){/tex}
{tex}=4 \sqrt{3} \mathrm{cm}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} each side of an equilateral = {tex}4 \sqrt{3} \mathrm{cm}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Area {tex}=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4} \times a^{2}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4} \times(4 \sqrt{3})^{2}{/tex}
{tex}=12 \sqrt{3} \mathrm{cm}^{2}{/tex}

k=-1

When we multiply two different root number then only number is multiplied not the root because we have both number power equal,

Or,  {tex}a^{\frac{1}{2}} \times b^{\frac{1}{2}}{/tex} = {tex}(ab)^{\frac{1}{2}}{/tex}

questions.question: If {tex}x=\frac{2}{3+\sqrt{7}}{/tex}, then (x - 3)2
questions.explanation:
{tex}x=\frac{2}{3+\sqrt{7}}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{2}{3+\sqrt{7}}\times \frac{3-\sqrt{7}}{3-\sqrt{7}}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{2(3-\sqrt{7})}{(3)^2-(\sqrt{7})^2}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{2(3-\sqrt{7})}{9-7}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{2(3-\sqrt{7})}{2}{/tex}
= 3 - {tex}\sqrt{7}{/tex}
Now (x - 3)2 = ( 3 - {tex}\sqrt{7}{/tex} -3)2
= (-{tex}\sqrt{7}{/tex})2
=7

questions.question: In the given figure, AB {tex}\parallel {/tex} CD. If {tex}\angle {/tex}AOC = 30° and {tex}\angle {/tex}OAB = 100°. then {tex}\angle {/tex}OCD =?
questions.explanation:
Draw {tex}O E\|A B\| C D{/tex}
Now, {tex}O E \| A B{/tex} and OA is the transversal.
{tex}\therefore \angle O A B+\angle A O E=180^{\circ}{/tex} [Angles on the same side of a transversal line are supplementary]
{tex}\Rightarrow \angle O A B+\angle A O C+\angle C O E=180^{\circ}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 100^{\circ}+30^{\circ}+\angle C O E=180^{\circ}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \angle C O E=50^{\circ}{/tex}
Also,
{tex}O E \| C D{/tex} and OC is the transversal.
{tex}\therefore \angle O C D+\angle C O E=180^{\circ}{/tex} [Angles on the same side of a transversal line are supplementary]
{tex}\Rightarrow \angle O C D+50^{\circ}=180^{\circ}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \angle O C D=130^{\circ}{/tex}

= {tex}\sqrt {15\left( {15 - 5} \right)\left( {15 - 12} \right)\left( {15 - 13} \right)} {/tex} 
= {tex}\sqrt {15 \times 10 \times 3 \times 2} {/tex} 
= 30 sq. cm

questions.question: The mean of n observations is {tex}\overline X{/tex}. If each observation is multiplied by k, the mean of new observations is:
questions.explanation:
Let us take n observations Xi, ..., Xn 
If {tex}\overline X{/tex} be the mean of the n observations, then we have
{tex}\overline X{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{n} \sum_\limits{i=1}^{n} X_{i}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\sum_\limits{i=1}^{n} X_{i}=n \overline{X}{/tex} 
Multiply a constant k to each of the observations. Then the observations becomes kXi, .... kXn.
If {tex}\overline Y{/tex} be the mean of the new observations, then we have
{tex}\overline Y{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{n} \sum_\limits{i=1}^{n} k X_{i}{/tex} 
= {tex}\frac{k}{n} \sum_\limits{i=1}^{n} X_{i}{/tex} 
= {tex}k \cdot \frac{1}{n} \sum_\limits{i=1}^{n} X_{i}{/tex} 
= k{tex}\overline X{/tex}

questions.question: If the perimeter and base of an isosceles triangle are 11 cm and 5 cm respectively, then its area is
questions.explanation:
Let each of the equal sides be x cm. Then
x + x + 5 = 11
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x = 6
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 3 cm
s = {tex}\frac{3 + 3 + 5} {2} = \frac{11}{2}{/tex} cm
Area = {tex}\sqrt {s\left( {s - a} \right)\left( {s - b} \right)\left( {s - c} \right)} {/tex} 
= {tex}\sqrt{\frac{11}{2}\left(\frac{11}{2}-3\right)\left(\frac{11}{2}-3\right)\left(\frac{11}{2}-5\right)}{/tex} 
= {tex}\sqrt{\frac{11}{2} \times \frac{5}{2} \times \frac{5}{2} \times \frac{1}{2}}{/tex} 
= {tex}\frac{5}{4}\sqrt {11} {/tex} sq. cm

questions.question: If the mean of x and {tex}\frac 1x{/tex} is M, then the mean of x3 and {tex}\frac {1} {x^3}{/tex} is
questions.explanation:
Given, {tex}\frac{x+\frac{1}{x}}{2}{/tex} = M
Taking cube on both sides
{tex}\left(\frac{x+\frac{1}{x}}{2}\right)^{3}{/tex} = (M)3 
{tex}\left(x+\frac{1}{x}\right)^{3}{/tex} = (2M)3 
{tex}\left(x^{3}+3x\times\frac1x\left(x+\frac{1}{x}\right)+\frac{1}{x^{3}}\right){/tex} = (2M)3 
{tex}\left(x^{3}+\frac{1}{x^{3}}\right){/tex} = 8M3 - 3{tex}\left(x+\frac{1}{x}\right){/tex} 
Divide by 2 on both sides to get mean
{tex}\frac{\left(x^{3}+\frac{1}{x^{3}}\right)}{2}{/tex} = 4M3 - {tex}{\frac{3}{2}}(x+\frac{1}{x}){/tex} 
{tex}\frac{\left(x^{3}+\frac{1}{x^{3}}\right)}{2}{/tex} = 4M3 - 3M

questions.question: If {tex}x = 3 + \sqrt 8 {/tex} , then the value of {tex}\left(x^2+\frac{1}{x^2}\right){/tex} is
questions.explanation:
Given: x = {tex}(3+\sqrt{8}){/tex}
{tex}\frac{1}{x}=\frac{1}{(3+\sqrt{8})}=\frac{1}{(3+\sqrt{8})} \times \frac{(3-\sqrt{8})}{(3-\sqrt{8})}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{(3-\sqrt{8})}{\left(3^2-(\sqrt{8})^2\right)}=\frac{(3+\sqrt{8})}{(9-8)}=(3-\sqrt{8}){/tex}
{tex}\left(x+\frac{1}{x}\right)=(3+\sqrt{8})+(3-\sqrt{8})=6{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow\left(x+\frac{1}{x}\right)^2=6^2{/tex} = 36
{tex}\Rightarrow\left(x^2+\frac{1}{x^2}\right)+2 \times x \times \frac{1}{x}=36{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow\left(x^2+\frac{1}{x^2}\right)+2=36{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow\left(x^2+\frac{1}{x^2}\right)=36-2{/tex} = 34

a2 = h2 + (a/2)2

questions.explanation:

{tex}\therefore{/tex} 110o + {tex}\angle{/tex}BAE = 180o
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}BAE = 180o - 110o = 70o
In {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABE

{tex}\therefore{/tex} 130o = 70o + xo
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} xo = 130o - 70o = 60o
Thus, the measure of angle {tex}\angle{/tex}ABC is 60o

questions.question: If side of a scalene {tex}\triangle {/tex} is doubled then area would be increased by
questions.explanation:
Area of triangle with sides a, b, c (A) = {tex}\sqrt {s\left( {s - a} \right)\left( {s - b} \right)\left( {s - c} \right)} {/tex}
New sides are 2a, 2b and 2c
Then {tex}s' = {{2a + 2b + 2c} \over 2} = a + b + c{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} s' = 2s .....(i)
New area = {tex}\sqrt {s'\left( {s' - 2a} \right)\left( {s' - 2b} \right)\left( {s' - 2c} \right)} {/tex}
= {tex}\sqrt {2s\left( {2s - 2a} \right)\left( {2s - 2b} \right)\left( {2s - 2c} \right)} {/tex}
= {tex}4\sqrt {s\left( {s - a} \right)\left( {s - b} \right)\left( {s - c} \right)} {/tex}
= 4A
Increased area = 4A - A = 3A
% of increased area = {tex}{{3{\rm{A}}} \over {\rm{A}}} \times 100{/tex} = 300%

options.text: Both Histogram is same as bar graph but joined together and no difference are correct
options.html: Both Histogram is same as bar graph but joined together and no difference are correct

questions.explanation:
A bar chart is used in summarizing categorical data. It uses a number of rectangles, of (arbitrary) equal width, corresponding to each category and have heights equal to number of cases in each category. Bar charts can be drawn horizontally or vertically, usually with gaps between rectangles.
While a histogram is used when data are measured in intervals; discrete or continuous. It illustrates the distribution of the data in a convenient form. Usually convenient if number of observations is very large, ( > ~ 100). Here width of rectangle is decided by the range of data values and can be varied, (but fixed in a plot) and are usually drawn without gaps. Therefore the area of the rectangle is key factor in histograms. Histograms are appropriate for numerical variables only and are helpful in identifying gaps in observations.

questions.question: In the given figure, two rays BD and CE intersect at a point A. The side BC of {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC have been produced on both sides to points F and G respectively. questions.explanation:
In the given figure, {tex}\angle A B F+\angle A B C=180^{\circ}{/tex} (Linear pair of angles)
{tex}\therefore x^{\circ}+\angle \mathrm{ABC}=180^{\circ}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \angle A B C=180^{\circ}-x^{\circ}{/tex}.....(1)
Also, {tex}\angle A C G+\angle A C B=180^{\circ}{/tex} (Linear pair of angles)
{tex}\therefore y^{\prime}+\angle A C B=180^{\circ}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \angle A C B=180^{\circ}-y^{\prime}{/tex} .....(2)
Also, {tex}\angle B A C=\angle D A E=z^{\circ}{/tex} .....(3) (Vertically opposite angles)
In {tex}\triangle ABC{/tex}
{tex}\angle B A C+\angle A B C+\angle A C B=180^{\circ}{/tex} (Angle sum property)
{tex}\therefore z^{0}+180^{\circ}-x^{0}+180^{\circ}-y^{\circ}=180^{\circ}{/tex}    [Using (1),(2) and (3)]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} z = x + y - 180

questions.question: The decimal representation of a rational number is
questions.explanation:
Rational numbers can be represented in decimal forms rather than representing in fractions. They can easily be represented as decimals by just dividing numerator ‘p’ by denominator ‘q’ (as rational numbers is in the form of p/q).

A rational number can be expressed as a terminating or nonterminating, recurring decimal.

For example:

(i) {tex}5\over 2{/tex} = 2.5, 

{tex}2\over 8{/tex}= 0.25, 

7 = 7.0, etc., are rational numbers which are terminating decimals.

(ii) {tex}5 \over 9{/tex}= 0.555555555……. = 0.5 ̇,

{tex}1 \over 6{/tex} = 0.166666 ….. = 0.16 ̇

{tex}9 \over 11{/tex}= 0.818181…… = 0.81  ̇etc., are rational numbers which are nonterminating, recurring decimals.

questions.question: If a linear equation has solutions (1, 2), (-1, -16) and (0, -7), then it is of the form

2 = 9 - 7
2 = 2
Hence (2, 1) is a solution.
For point (-1, -16)
y = 9x - 7
-16 = 9(-1) - 7
-16 = -9 - 7
-16 = -16
Hence (-1, -16) is a solution.
For point (0,-7)
y = 9x - 7
-7 = 9(0) -7
-7 = -7
Hence (0, -7) is a solution.

questions.question: The algebraic sum of the deviations of a set of n values from their mean is:
questions.explanation:
If {tex}\overline X{/tex} be the mean of the n observations q Xi, ..., Xn then we have
{tex}\overline X{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{n} \sum_\limits{i=1}^{n} X_{i}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\sum_\limits{i=1}^{n} X_{i}=n \bar{X}{/tex} 
Let {tex}\overline X{/tex} be the mean of n values Xi, ..., Xn. So, we have
{tex}\overline X{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{n} \sum_\limits{i=1}^{n} X_{i}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\sum_\limits{i=1}^{n} X_{i}=n \bar{X}{/tex} 
The sum of the deviations of n values Xi, ..., Xn from their mean {tex}\overline X{/tex} is
(x1 - {tex}\overline X{/tex}) + (x2 - {tex}\overline X{/tex}) + ...+ (xn - {tex}\overline X{/tex})
= {tex}\sum_\limits{i=1}^{n}\left(x_{i}-\bar{X}\right){/tex} 
= {tex}\sum_\limits{i=1}^{n} x_{i}-\sum_\limits{i=1}^{n} \bar{X}{/tex} 
= n{tex}\overline X{/tex} - n{tex}\overline X{/tex}
= 0

questions.question: The height of an equilateral triangle is 6 cm, Then the area of the triangle is
questions.explanation:
Let each side of an equilateral triangle be 'a' units.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} its height {tex}=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \times \mathrm{a}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore 6=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \times \mathrm{a}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore a=\frac{12}{\sqrt{3}}{/tex}
{tex}=\left(\frac{12}{\sqrt{3}} \times \frac{\sqrt{3}}{\sqrt{3}}\right){/tex} ..... (Rationalizing the denominator)
{tex}=\left(\frac{12 \sqrt{3}}{3}\right){/tex}
{tex}=4 \sqrt{3} \mathrm{cm}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} each side of an equilateral = {tex}4 \sqrt{3} \mathrm{cm}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Area {tex}=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4} \times a^{2}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4} \times(4 \sqrt{3})^{2}{/tex}
{tex}=12 \sqrt{3} \mathrm{cm}^{2}{/tex}

Upper limit = x + 10

x = 37 = lower limit
x + 10 = 47 = upper limit
Thus, upper and lower limits are 47, 37

questions.question: In the figure, {tex}A B \| C D{/tex}, EG & FG are Bisectors of {tex}\angle B E F \ \& \ \angle D F E{/tex} respectively, then {tex}\angle F G E{/tex} is:
questions.explanation:
90o
Since interior angles on the same sides of a transversal are supplementary, we have
{tex}\angle{/tex}BEF + {tex}\angle{/tex} EFD = 180
{tex}\angle{/tex} BEG + {tex}\angle{/tex} GEF + {tex}\angle{/tex} EFG +{tex}\angle{/tex} GFD = 180
As, {tex}\angle{/tex} BEG = {tex}\angle{/tex} GEF, and {tex}\angle{/tex} EFG = {tex}\angle{/tex} GFD, we have
{tex}\angle{/tex} GEF + {tex}\angle{/tex} GEF + {tex}\angle{/tex} EFG + {tex}\angle{/tex} EFG = 180
2 {tex}\angle{/tex} GEF + 2 {tex}\angle{/tex} EFG = 180
2 ({tex}\angle{/tex} GEF + {tex}\angle{/tex} EFG) = 180
{tex}\angle{/tex} GEF + {tex}\angle{/tex} EFG = 90 
Now, by angle sum property of triangles, we have
{tex}\angle{/tex} GEF + {tex}\angle{/tex} EFG + {tex}\angle{/tex} EGF = 180
90 + {tex}\angle{/tex} EGF = 180
{tex}\angle{/tex}  EGF = 90

questions.question: The width of each of nine classes in a frequency distribution is 2.5 and the lower class boundary of the lowest class 10.6. Then the upper class boundary of the highest class is
questions.explanation:
Number of classes = 9
Lower limit of the lowest class = 10.6
Width of each class = 2.5
So, Upper limit of the lowest class = 10.6 + 2.5 = 13.1
Now, Upper limit of the lowest class + Width of each class = Upper limit of the next class
Thus, we have
Upper limit of the lowest class + 8 × width of each class = Upper limit of the highest (9th) class
Upper limit of the highest (9th) class = 13.1 + 8 {tex}\times{/tex} 2.5 = 33.1

questions.question: The taxi fare in a city is as follows: For the first kilometer, the fare is ₹8 and for the subsequent distance it is ₹5 per kilometer. Taking the distance covered as x km and total fare as ₹y, write a linear equation for this information.
questions.explanation:
Taxi fare for first kilometer = ₹8
Taxi fare for subsequent distance = ₹5
Total distance covered = x
Total fare = y
Since the fare for first kilometer = ₹8
According to problem, Fare for (x - 1) kilometer = 5(x - 1)
So, the total fare y = 5(x - 1) + 8
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = 5(x - 1) + 8
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = 5x – 5 + 8
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = 5x + 3 
Hence, y = 5x + 3 is the required linear equation.

{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 7
Therefore, the sides are 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 7 = 21, cm, 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 7 = 28 cm and 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 7 = 35 cm
s = {tex}{{21 + 28 + 35} \over 2}{/tex} = 42 cm
Area of triangle = {tex}\sqrt {s\left( {s - a} \right)\left( {s - b} \right)\left( {s - c} \right)} {/tex} 
= {tex}\sqrt {42\left( {42 - 21} \right)\left( {42 - 28} \right)\left( {42 - 35} \right)} {/tex} 
= {tex}\sqrt {42 \times 21 \times 14 \times 7} {/tex} 
= 21 {tex}\times{/tex} 7 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 = 294 sq. cm

{tex}\angle{/tex}ABC = 180° - 110°
{tex}\angle{/tex}ABC = 70°
In ABC

{tex}\angle{/tex}ACB = 65°

questions.question: The sides of a triangle are in the ratio 5 : 12 : 13 and its perimeter is 150 cm. The area of the triangle is
questions.explanation:
Let the sides of the triangle be 5x cm, 12x cm and 13x cm.
Perimeter = Sum of all sides
or, 150 = 5x + 12x + 13x
or, 30x = 150
or, x = 5
Thus, the sides of the triangle are 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 5 cm, 12 {tex}\times{/tex} 5 cm and 13 {tex}\times{/tex} 5 cm, i.e., 25 cm, 60 cm and 65 cm.
Now,
Let:
a = 25 cm, b = 60 cm and c = 65 cm
{tex}s=\frac{150}{2}=75 \mathrm{cm}{/tex}
By Heron's formula, we have:
Area of triangle {tex}=\sqrt{s(s-a)(s-b)(s-c)}{/tex}
{tex}=\sqrt{75(75-25)(75-60)(75-65)}{/tex}
{tex}=\sqrt{75 \times 50 \times 15 \times 10}{/tex}
{tex}=\sqrt{15 \times 5 \times 5 \times 10 \times 15 \times 10}{/tex}
{tex}=15 \times 5 \times 10{/tex}
= 750 cm2

questions.question: If one of the angles of a triangle is 130°, then the angle between the bisectors of the other two angles can be
questions.explanation:
Let angles of a triangle be {tex}\angle{/tex}A, {tex}\angle{/tex}B and {tex}\angle{/tex}C. 

In {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC,
{tex}\angle A+\angle B+\angle C=180^{\circ}{/tex} [sum of all interior angles of a triangle is 180o] 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{2} \angle A+\frac{1}{2} \angle B+\frac{1}{2} \angle C=\frac{180^{\circ}}{2}=90^{\circ}{/tex} [dividing both sides by 2]
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{2} \angle B+\frac{1}{2} \angle C=90^{\circ}-\frac{1}{2} \angle A{/tex}  [{tex}\because{/tex} In {tex}\triangle{/tex}OBC, {tex}\angle{/tex}OBC +{tex}\angle{/tex}BCO + {tex}\angle{/tex}COB = 180o]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Since, {tex}\frac{\angle B}{2}+\frac{\angle C}{2}+\angle B O C=180^{\circ}{/tex} as BO and OC are the angle bisectors of {tex}\angle{/tex}ABC and {tex}\angle{/tex}BCA, respectively
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}180^{\circ}-\angle B O C=90^{\circ}-\frac{1}{2} \angle A{/tex} 
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}BOC = 180o - 90o + {tex}\frac{1}{2}\angle A{/tex} 
= {tex}90^{\circ}+\frac{1}{2} \times 130^{\circ}{/tex} = 90o + 65o [{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}A = 130o (given)]
= 155o 
Hence, the required angle is 155o.

questions.question: The mean of n observations is {tex}\overline X{/tex}. If k is added to each observation, then the new mean is:
questions.explanation:
Let us take n observations X1, ... Xn 
If {tex}\overline X{/tex} be the mean of the n observations, then we have
{tex}\overline X{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{n} \sum_\limits{i=1}^{n} X_{i}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\sum_\limits{i=1}^{n} X_{i}{/tex} = n{tex}\overline X{/tex} 
Add a constant k to each of the observations. Then the observations becomes Xi + k, ..., Xn + k
If {tex}\overline Y{/tex} be the mean of the new observations, then we have
{tex}\overline Y{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{n} \sum_\limits{i=1}^{n}{/tex} (Xi + k) 
= {tex}\frac{1}{n} \sum_\limits{i=1}^{n} X_{i}+\frac{1}{n} \sum_\limits{i=1}^{n} k{/tex} 
= {tex}\overline{X}+\frac{1}{n} \cdot n k{/tex} 
= {tex}\overline X{/tex} + k

questions.question: In the given figure, side BC of {tex}\triangle ABC{/tex} has been produced to a point D. If {tex}\angle A=3 y^{\circ}{/tex} {tex}\angle B=x^{\circ}{/tex}, {tex}\angle C=5 y^{\circ} {/tex} and {tex}\angle C B D=7 y^{\circ}{/tex} . Then, the value of x is
questions.explanation:
In the given figure {tex}\angle A C B+\angle A C D=180^{\circ}{/tex} (Linear pair of angles)
{tex}\therefore 5 y^{\prime}+7 y^{\prime}=180^{\circ}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 12 y^{\circ}=180^{\circ}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = 15 ....(i)
In {tex}\triangle \mathrm{ABC}{/tex},
{tex}\angle A+\angle B+\angle A C B=180^{\circ}{/tex} (angle sum property)
{tex}\therefore 3 y^{\prime}+x^{\prime}+5 y^{\prime}=180^{\circ}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow x^{\circ}+8 y^{\circ}=180^{\circ}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow x^{2}+8 \times 15^{\circ}=180^{\circ}{/tex} [using (1)]
{tex}\Rightarrow x^{\prime}+120^{\circ}=180^{\circ}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow x^{\circ}=180^{\circ}-120^{\circ}=60^{\circ}{/tex}
Thus, the value of x is 60.

questions.question: If x= {tex}\frac{\sqrt{5}+\sqrt{3}}{\sqrt{5}-\sqrt{3}}{/tex} and {tex}y=\frac{\sqrt{5}-\sqrt{3}}{\sqrt{5}+\sqrt{3}}{/tex} , then x + y + xy =

Then,
x + y + xy =  {tex}\frac{\sqrt{5}+\sqrt{3}}{\sqrt{5}-\sqrt{3}}{/tex}+ {tex}\frac{\sqrt{5}-\sqrt{3}}{\sqrt{5}+\sqrt{3}}{/tex}+{tex}\frac{\sqrt{5}+\sqrt{3}}{\sqrt{5}-\sqrt{3}}​​\times \frac{\sqrt{5}-\sqrt{3}}{\sqrt{5}+\sqrt{3}} {/tex}
={tex}\frac{\sqrt{5}+\sqrt{3}}{\sqrt{5}-\sqrt{3}}{/tex}{tex}\times{/tex}{tex}\frac{\sqrt{5}+\sqrt{3}}{\sqrt{5}+\sqrt{3}}{/tex} + {tex}\frac{\sqrt{5}-\sqrt{3}}{\sqrt{5}+\sqrt{3}}{/tex}{tex}\times{/tex}{tex}\frac{\sqrt{5}-\sqrt{3}}{\sqrt{5}-\sqrt{3}}{/tex}+ 1
= {tex}\frac{(\sqrt{5}+\sqrt{3})^2}{5-3}{/tex}+ {tex}\frac{(\sqrt{5}-\sqrt{3})^2}{5-3}{/tex} +1
= {tex}\frac{(\sqrt{5})^2+(\sqrt{3})^2+2(\sqrt{5})(\sqrt{3)}}{2}{/tex}+ {tex}\frac{(\sqrt{5})^2+(\sqrt{3})^2-2(\sqrt{5})(\sqrt{3)}}{2}{/tex}+1
= {tex}\frac{5+3+2\sqrt{15}}{2}{/tex}+ {tex}\frac{5+3-2\sqrt{15}}{2}{/tex}+1
= {tex}\frac{8+2\sqrt{15}}{2}{/tex}+ {tex}\frac{8-2\sqrt{15}}{2}{/tex}+1
= 4 + {tex}\sqrt{15}{/tex} + 4 - {tex}\sqrt{15}{/tex} +1
= 8+1
=9

{tex}\sqrt{\frac{(\sqrt{2}-1)}{(\sqrt{2}+1)}}{/tex}
= {tex}\sqrt{\frac{(\sqrt{2}-1)}{(\sqrt{2}+1)} \times \frac{(\sqrt{2}-1)}{(\sqrt{2}-1)}}{/tex}
= {tex}\sqrt{\frac{(\sqrt{2}-1)^{2}}{(\sqrt{2})^{2}-(1)^{2}}}{/tex}
={tex}\sqrt{\frac{(\sqrt{2}-1)^{2}}{2-1}}{/tex}
={tex}\sqrt{(\sqrt{2}-1)^{2}} {/tex}
= 1.414 - 1

questions.question: The sides of a triangle are 35 cm, 54 cm and 61 cm, respectively. The length of its longest altitude
questions.explanation:
Let ABC be a triangle in which sides AB = 35cm, BC = 54 cm and CA = 61 cm

Now, semi-perimeter of a triangle,
{tex}s=\frac{a+b+c}{2}{/tex}{tex}=\frac{35+54+61}{2}=\frac{150}{2}=75 \mathrm{cm}{/tex}
[{tex}\because{/tex} semiperimeter, s = {tex}\frac{a+b+c}{2}{/tex}]
{tex}\because{/tex} Area of {tex}\triangle {/tex}ABC {tex}=\sqrt{s(s-a)(s-b)(s-c)}{/tex} [by Heron's formula]
{tex}=\sqrt{75(75-35)(75-54)(75-61)}{/tex}
{tex}=\sqrt{75 \times 40 \times 21 \times 14}{/tex}
{tex}=\sqrt{25 \times 3 \times 4 \times 2 \times 5 \times 7 \times 3 \times 7 \times 2}{/tex}
{tex}=5 \times 2 \times 2 \times 3 \times 7 \sqrt{5}{/tex}
{tex}=420 \sqrt{5} \mathrm{cm}^{2}{/tex}
Also, Area of {tex}\triangle A B C=\frac{1}{2} \times A B \times \text { Altitude }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{2} \times 35 \times C D{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow C D=\frac{420 \times 2 \sqrt{5}}{35}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} CD {tex}=24 \sqrt{5}{/tex}
Hence, the length of altitude is {tex}24 \sqrt{5} \mathrm{cm}{/tex}.

questions.question: If g = {tex}t^{\frac 2 3} + 4t^ {\frac {-1} 2}{/tex}, what is the value of g when t = 64?
questions.explanation:
g = {tex}t^{\frac 2 3} + 4t^ {\frac {-1} 2}{/tex}
= {tex}t^{\frac{2}{3}}{/tex} + 4 {tex}\times \frac{1}{t^{\frac{1}{2}}}{/tex}
= {tex}(64)^{\frac{2}{3}}{/tex} + 4 {tex}\times \frac{1}{64^{\frac{1}{2}}}{/tex}
= {tex}\left(4^{3}\right)^{\frac{2}{3}}{/tex} + 4 {tex}\times \frac{1}{\left(8^{2}\right)^{\frac{1}{2}}} {/tex}
= {tex}4^{\frac{2}{3} \times 3}{/tex} + 4 {tex}\times \frac{1}{8^{2 \times \frac{1}{2}}} {/tex}
= 42 + {tex}\frac{4}{8} {/tex}
= 16 + {tex}\frac{1}{2} {/tex}
= {tex}\frac{33}{2} {/tex}

questions.question: Which of the points A(-5, 0), B(0, -3), C(3, 0), D(0, 4) are closer to the origin?
questions.explanation:
Points B and C both
As per distance formulla, the distance between the points P(a, b) and Q(x, y) is given by:
|PQ| = {tex}\sqrt {(x-a)^2+(y-b)^2}{/tex}
If O(0. 0) be the origin, then we have
|AO| = {tex}\sqrt {(-5)^2}=5{/tex}
|BO| = {tex}\sqrt {(-3)^2}=3{/tex}
|CO| = {tex}\sqrt {3^2}=3{/tex}
|DO| = {tex}\sqrt {(4)^2}=4{/tex}
This shows that B and C are both at minimum distance from the origin.

questions.question:

  2. A triangle can have two right angles.
  3. A triangle can have two obtuse angles.

questions.explanation: A triangle can have two or even all three acute angles (in case of an equilateral triangle) but it cannot have two right angles or two obtuse angles as the sum of the interior angles of a triangle is 180o and two right angles or two obtuse angles would sum up to 180o or more leaving the no space for the third angle.

questions.question: The area of a triangle two sides of which are 18cm and 10 cm and its perimeter is 42 cm will be-
questions.explanation:
First side of the triangle = a  = 18 cm
Second side of the triangle = b = 10cm
Third side of the triangle = c
a + b = 18 + 10 = 28 cm
Perimeter of the triangle = a + b + c = 42 cm
Hence,
c = 42 cm - 28 cm = 14 cm
Also,
{tex}s=\frac{a+b+c}{2}=\frac{42}{2}=21{/tex}
Now, by Heron's formula,
Area of the triangle = A {tex}=\sqrt{s(s-a)(s-b)(s-c)}{/tex}
A = {tex}\sqrt{21(21-18)(21-10)(21-14)}{/tex}
A = {tex}\sqrt{21 \times 3 \times 11 \times 7}{/tex}

questions.question: The sides of a triangle are 16 cm, 30 cm, 34 cm. Its area is
questions.explanation:
Let a = 16 cm, b = 30 cm, c = 34 cm
Semi-perimeter of a triangle = {tex}\frac{a+b+c}{2}=\frac{16+30+34}{2}{/tex} = 40
Now, s - a = 24 cm, s - b = 10 cm and s - c = 6 cm
By Heron's formula. Area of a triangle = {tex}\sqrt{s(s-a)(s-b)(s-c)}{/tex}
= {tex}\sqrt{40 \times 24 \times 10 \times 6}{/tex}
= {tex}\sqrt{4 \times 10 \times 4 \times 6 \times 10 \times 6} {/tex}
= {tex}\sqrt{4^{2} \times 10^{2} \times 6^{2}} {/tex}
= 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 {tex}\times{/tex} 6
= 240 cm2

questions.question: The height corresponding to the longest side of the triangle whose sides are 42 cm, 34 cm and 20 cm in length is.

Area {tex}=\sqrt{S(S-a)(S-b)(S-c)}{/tex}
{tex}=\sqrt{48(48-42)(48-34)(48-20)}{/tex}
{tex}=\sqrt{48 \times 6 \times 14 \times 28}{/tex}

{tex}=4 \times 3 \times 2 \times 14{/tex}
= 336 cm2
Longest side = 42 cm
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} b = 42 cm
Let h be the height corresponding to the longest side.
Area of the triangle {tex}=\frac{1}{2} \times b \times h{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{2} \times b \times h=336{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 42 \times h=336 \times 2{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow h=\frac{336 \times 2}{42}=16 \mathrm{cm}{/tex}

questions.question:
In the given figure, lines AB and CD intersect at a point O. The sides CA and OB have been produced to E and F respectively such that {tex}\angle{/tex}OAE = x° and {tex}\angle{/tex}DBF = y°.

If {tex}\angle O C A=80^{\circ}{/tex}, {tex}\angle C O A=40^{\circ}{/tex} and {tex}\angle B D O=70^{\circ}{/tex} then xo + yo = ?
questions.explanation:
In the given figure, {tex}\angle{/tex} BOD = {tex}\angle{/tex}COA (Vertically opposite angles)
{tex}\therefore \angle B O D=40^{\circ}{/tex} ....(1)
In {tex}\triangle \mathrm{ACO}{/tex}
{tex}\angle O A E=\angle O C A+\angle C O A{/tex} (Exterior angle of a triangle is equal to the sum of two opposite interior angles)
{tex}\Rightarrow x^{\circ}=80^{\circ}+40^{\circ}=120^{\circ}{/tex} .....(2)
In {tex}\angle BDO{/tex},
{tex}\angle D B F=\angle B D O+\angle B O D{/tex}  (Exterior angle of a triangle is equal to the sum of two opposite interior angles)
{tex}\Rightarrow y^{\circ}=70^{\circ}+40^{\circ}=110^{\circ}{/tex} [Unsing (1)] ...(3)
Adding  (2) and (3) we get
{tex}x^{\circ}+y^{\circ}=120^{\circ}+110^{\circ}=230^{\circ}{/tex}
Hence the correct option is 230o
